prompt_text: Open source or affordable automation tools instead of Make
response:
The most popular **open source or low-cost alternatives to Make.com** for general workflow and app automation are **n8n, Automatisch, Pipedream (open-core), Huginn, and ActiveWorkflow**, plus low-cost SaaS options like **Integrately** and **IFTTT**.[1][3]

Below are the best options, with a quick “when to use” for each.

---

### 1. n8n – open‑source, very close to Make’s style

- **Type:** Source-available / open-core workflow automation[1][3]  
- **How it compares to Make:**  
  - Similar **visual node-based editor** for flows.  
  - Large and growing list of integrations; good for APIs, webhooks, AI tools, etc.[1][3]  
- **Cost:**  
  - **Self-hosted:** free.  
  - Hosted cloud: paid tiers, but generally competitive vs Make.  
- **Best for:**  
  - Users who like Make’s visual editor but want **self‑hosting**, more control, and no vendor lock‑in.[1]

---

### 2. Automatisch – open source “Zapier/Make-like” app connector

- **Type:** Fully **open‑source** (AGPL‑3.0) business automation tool[3][4]  
- **What it does:**  
  - Connects services like **Slack, Twitter, etc.** to automate business processes without coding.[4]  
  - Marketed explicitly as an **alternative to Zapier/Integromat (Make)**.[4]  
- **Cost:**  
  - **Community Edition:** free, self-hosted.[4]  
  - Also offers an **affordable cloud** option.[3]  
- **Best for:**  
  - Small teams that want a **self-hosted, Make-style tool** with a simple UI and common SaaS integrations, and that want to keep costs down.

---

### 3. Pipedream – developer‑oriented open-source alternative

- **Type:** Open-source / open-core workflow platform focused on developers[3]  
- **Strengths:**  
  - Great for **API-based workflows**, serverless-style tasks, and integrating AI, databases, and services in code.[3]  
- **Cost:**  
  - Generous free tier; paid tiers scale with usage.  
- **Best for:**  
  - Technical users comfortable with JavaScript/TypeScript who want an **automation + integration platform with more code-level control** than Make.[1][3]

---

### 4. Huginn – self‑hosted “DIY IFTTT/Zapier”

- **Type:** Open-source agent system for “if this then that” style automations[3]  
- **What it does:**  
  - Lets you build **agents that watch the web (feeds, APIs, websites)** and trigger actions; described as a **self-hosted, hackable version of IFTTT/Zapier**.[3]  
- **Cost:**  
  - Completely free; you run it yourself.  
- **Best for:**  
  - Developers and power users who want **full data control**, custom logic, and are comfortable with a less-polished UI.

---

### 5. ActiveWorkflow – workflow engine for services & microservices

- **Type:** Open-source workflow automation platform[3]  
- **What it does:**  
  - Orchestrates workflows across **services, APIs, and microservices**; focused more on **backend and engineering teams** than no‑code users.[3]  
- **Cost:**  
  - Free to self-host.  
- **Best for:**  
  - Engineering/DevOps teams that want a **structured, reliable orchestration layer** without vendor lock‑in, rather than a marketing-friendly no‑code tool.[3]

---

### 6. Low‑cost SaaS options (not open source but cheaper than Make)

If open source isn’t strictly required and you mainly want **affordable**:

- **Integrately** – automation platform with many SaaS integrations, positioned as **budget-friendly** and a good fit especially for **e‑commerce businesses** transitioning from Make.[2]  
- **IFTTT** – very low-cost plans (from a few dollars/month), great for **simple personal or home automations** and some business workflows.[2]  

---

### 7. AI-focused automation tools that can replace some Make use cases

Depending on what you do in Make, AI-first tools might be relevant:

- **Gumloop** – AI-powered automation for **AI workflows and AI agents**, used as a Make alternative for AI-heavy use cases.[2]  
- **Langflow, Relay.app, Lindy AI** – focus on **LLM + tool workflows**, good if your main use of Make is around AI/chatbot/agent orches
